sections.summary: Scope: this standard applies to the modified stems (nopals) of specified Opuntia species supplied fresh to consumers (commercial varieties only) and excludes nopals intended for industrial processing.

Sets minimum quality requirements for nopals — they must be whole, sound (no rot), clean, practically free of pest damage and visible foreign matter, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign tastes/odours, firm, free of low‑temperature damage and prickles, display satisfactory development and ripeness, and be characteristic in shape, colour, taste and smell.

Minimum requirements reiterate that nopals must meet criteria such as being whole, sound, clean, practically free of pest damage and foreign matter, free of abnormal moisture, odour or prickles, firm, properly developed and characteristic of the species.

sections.section: 2.1.1  The development and condition of the nopals must be such as to enable them:
sections.summary: The development and condition of nopals must enable them to withstand transport and handling and to arrive in satisfactory condition at the place of destination.

Nopals are classified into three classes (Extra, Class I, Class II) with descending quality requirements: Extra is superior and nearly defect‑free, Class I allows slight defects not affecting appearance/quality, and Class II includes produce meeting only the minimum requirements with larger permitted defects.

'Extra' Class: nopals must be of superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I: nopals must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety/commercial type, with only slight defects allowed provided they do not affect general appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation; slight skin defects are permitted provided the total affected area does not exceed 5%.
Editorial note: the standard text was amended in 2005 (non‑substantive amendment indicator).

The Codex Standard specifies allowable slight defects (shape, colour, skin defects such as bruising, scarring or blemishes up to 5% area for certain classes) and defines Class II as produce not qualifying for higher classes but meeting minimum requirements, with skin defects allowed up to 10% area.

Class II: includes nopals that do not qualify for higher classes but satisfy minimum requirements, allowing defects in shape and colour and skin defects (bruising, scarring, crusting, spots) provided the total affected area does not exceed 10%.
Sizing is determined by nopal length with size codes: A 9–13 cm, B 13–17 cm, C 17–21 cm, D 21–25 cm, and E 25–30 cm.

Tolerances: quality tolerances allow by number or weight 5% for 'Extra' Class (not meeting class but meeting Class I), 10% for Class I (meeting Class II), and 10% for Class II (not meeting minimum requirements except produce unfit for consumption); size tolerances allow 5% for 'Extra' and 10% for Class I and II for produce outside size requirements but within adjacent class ranges.

Quality tolerances by class: 'Extra' allows 5% by number/weight not satisfying the class but meeting Class I tolerances; Class I allows 10% not satisfying the class but meeting Class II tolerances; Class II allows 10% that fail class requirements but are not unfit for consumption.
'Extra' Class tolerance: up to 5% by number or weight of nopals in a package may fail the class requirements provided they meet Class I or its tolerances.
Class I tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of nopals in a package may fail Class I requirements provided they meet Class II or its tolerances.

Allows up to 10% by number or weight of nopals that fail both the class requirements and the minimum requirements, excluding produce that is rotting, has pronounced irregularities, or is otherwise unfit for consumption.

Permits size tolerances for nopals of up to 5% (by number or weight) for 'Extra' Class and up to 10% for Class I or II for produce not meeting sizing but falling within the immediately adjacent class.
Section heading only (Provisions concerning presentation); no substantive provisions provided in this entry.

Requires each package or bulk lot of nopals to be uniform in origin, variety, quality and size (with 'Extra' Class also requiring uniform colour and ripeness) and that the visible portion be representative of the whole contents.

Requires nopals to be packed to protect the produce using clean (preferably new or food-grade recycled) materials that do not cause damage, permits printed materials only with non-toxic ink or glue, mandates compliance with CAC/RCP 44-1995, and requires containers to meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance standards.
Containers must provide adequate quality, hygiene, ventilation and strength to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation of nopals and packages must be free of foreign matter and odour.

In addition to Codex STAN 1-1985, specifies labelling requirements: consumer packages must show the produce name (and optionally variety) if not visible, and non-retail containers must bear exporter/packer/dispatcher identification, produce name/variety, origin, class, size, and optionally number of units and net weight, with an optional official inspection mark.
Introductory heading noting that additional labelling requirements supplementing Codex STAN 1-1985 apply to consumer packages; no further detail in this subsection.
If the produce is not visible from outside the package, consumer packages must be labelled with the name of the produce and may include the variety.

Non-retail containers must bear, legibly and indelibly or in accompanying documents for bulk shipments, specified particulars including identification, nature and origin of produce; accompanying notes address recycled food-grade materials and code-mark usage.
Requires the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher on non-retail containers, with an optional identification code.
Non-retail containers must state the name of the produce if contents are not visible and may indicate the variety or commercial type.

Requires declaration of country of origin (and optionally district or place name) and notes that packaging may include recycled food-grade material and that code marks must be linked to packer/dispatcher identification per national rules.
Mandates commercial identification on non-retail containers including class and size (size code or length range in centimetres) and optionally number of units and net weight.
Permits the use of an official inspection mark on packages; no further detail provided.
Requires that the produce comply with Codex limits for contaminants and toxins (CODEX STAN 193-1995) and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Recommends that nopals be prepared and handled in accordance with Codex Codes of Hygienic Practice (e.g., CAC/RCP 1 and CAC/RCP 53) and comply with any microbiological criteria established under CAC/GL 21-1997.
